subjectLine: Human MSTN mutations: +10% muscle, less fat in 1.1M 💪
borisTake: This is the clearest human validation yet that myostatin blockade is worth pursuing long-term — 1.1 million people, consistent muscle gains, no safety signals. With GLP-1s now stripping muscle off millions of patients, this target just moved from 'interesting research' to 'urgent clinical priority'.
leadAnalysis: The study pooled genetic data from 1.1 million individuals and found that carriers of function-disrupting MSTN variants show >10% increases in skeletal muscle mass across multiple groups, reduced adiposity, and higher grip strength — with no compensatory adverse phenotypes detected. The mechanism is well-established: myostatin signals through ActRIIB receptors to suppress satellite cell activation and muscle protein synthesis via Smad2/3 phosphorylation, and lifelong partial reduction replicates the lean, muscular phenotype seen in other species' knockout models. The clinical implication is direct: it validates the biology behind ActRIIB inhibitors like bimagrumab, currently in trials for both sarcopenia and GLP-1-induced muscle loss. Crucially, heterozygous carriers — with only ~50% reduction in myostatin function — still show robust, consistent benefits, suggesting you don't need complete blockade to achieve meaningful therapeutic effect.
oneNumber: 66% | Lower all-cause mortality for high-CV-risk adults vaccinated against shingles (123,411-person real-world study)

papers.title: Human MSTN Loss-of-Function Variants: +10% Muscle Mass, Reduced Adiposity in 1.1M-Person Study
papers.summary: Genetic analysis across 1.1 million individuals confirms that partial loss-of-function mutations in the myostatin gene (MSTN) produce consistent increases in skeletal muscle mass (>10% in heterozygotes), reduced body fat, and higher grip strength — with no adverse phenotypes. Whole-body MRI on 77,572 UK Biobank participants confirmed muscle gains across multiple muscle groups. Provides strong human validation for therapeutic myostatin blockade, especially relevant amid widespread GLP-1 use.

papers.title: Shingles Vaccine Cuts All-Cause Mortality 66% in High-CV-Risk Adults
papers.summary: Real-world analysis of 123,411 matched patients with atherosclerotic disease found shingles vaccination associated with 66% lower all-cause mortality, 46% lower MACE, 32% fewer heart attacks, and 25% fewer strokes at 1-year follow-up. Effect size is comparable to smoking cessation. Mechanism likely involves prevention of herpes zoster-triggered thromboinflammation and potential trained immunity effects reducing chronic inflammation.

papers.title: Partial Reprogramming Is Conserved from Insect to Mammal
papers.summary: A new study demonstrates that partial epigenetic reprogramming is evolutionarily conserved from insect models to mammals, strengthening the biological foundation for therapeutic reprogramming approaches. Conservation across such phylogenetically distant species implies the underlying epigenetic reset mechanisms are ancient and robust — key reassurance for clinical translation timelines.

papers.title: Sleep EEG Brain Age Index: 39% Higher Dementia Risk Per 10-Year Increase
papers.summary: A meta-analysis of 7,105 participants across 5 longitudinal cohorts found each 10-year increase in sleep EEG-derived brain age index (BAI) associated with 39% higher risk of incident dementia, independent of comorbidities, sleep apnea severity, and APOE4 status. The BAI is calculated from sleep microstructure EEG during standard polysomnography — making an overnight sleep study a potential dementia screening tool years before symptom onset.

papers.title: DeepStrataAge: Interpretable Deep-Learning Epigenetic Clock Reveals Sex-Divergent Aging Waves
papers.summary: A DNN-based DNA methylation clock trained on 29,167 samples achieves 1.89-year accuracy and outperforms existing models. SHAP analysis reveals three structured aging phases — early-life, midlife transition, late-life remodeling — with distinct sex-specific timing. Males show immune activation in late life while females show transcriptional remodeling. First methylation clock that's mechanistically interpretable rather than purely predictive.

papers.title: Two Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids (α-ESA) Show Senolytic Activity In Vitro and In Vivo
papers.summary: α-eleostearic acid (α-ESA) and its methyl ester were identified as senolytics in cell culture and a mouse model. Dietary conjugated fatty acids with senolytic properties opens a nutritional angle for senescence clearance alongside pharmacological approaches like navitoclax and dasatinib/quercetin.

papers.title: Amyloid-β Displaces Tau from Microtubules — A Mechanistic Rethink for Alzheimer's
papers.summary: Researchers propose that amyloid-β binds microtubules with affinity comparable to tau itself, and that competitive displacement of tau by Aβ drives tau phosphorylation and aggregation — without requiring amyloid aggregation as the primary toxin. This reframes the Aβ-tau relationship, explains why amyloid burden doesn't always track cognitive decline, and suggests new therapeutic targets at the microtubule interface upstream of tau pathology.
